metadata.description: Recall@K evaluation set generated from uploaded chunked_data.json. Treat a query as successful for Recall@K if any expected_chunk_id appears in the top K retrieved chunks.

data.question: What architecture benchmarks did POSET-RL evaluate, and what average SPEC 2017 improvements did the ODG model achieve?
data.answer_hint: The ODG model was evaluated on x86/AArch64 with SPEC CPU 2006/2017 and MiBench, achieving 6.19% size and 11.99% execution-time improvement on SPEC 2017.
data.expected_chunk_text: We show results on x86 and AArch64 architectures on the benchmarks from SPEC-CPU 2006, SPEC-CPU 2017 and MiBench. We observe that the proposed model based on ODG outperforms the current Oz sequence both in terms of size and execution time by 6.19% and 11.99% in SPEC 2017 benchmarks, on an average. Keywords-Phase Ordering, Compiler Optimization, Reinforce- ment learning I. INTRODUCTION One of the essential components of compiler construction are optimizations.They involve transforming the input pro- gram and generating code that is semantically equivalent to it, but performs better in various metrics like execution time, code size, power utilization, etc.

data.question: What trade-off does the paper describe between LLVM O3 and Oz optimization flags?
data.answer_hint: Oz improves code size by about 3.5% but incurs about 10% more execution time than O3.
data.expected_chunk_text: The Os flag is designed to perform nearly similar to O2 flag, while promising an appreciable decrease in code size. The Oz flag is designed to provide more code size reduction than Os. Such a reduction in code size by optimizations in Oz, generally incurs penalty on the execution time, leading to a trade-off between O3 and Oz in terms of code size and execution time.We observe on a set of benchmarks from the standard SPEC CPU benchmarks that the binaries generated by Oz flag incurs 10% more execution time than that of O3; while resulting in about 3.5% improvement in terms of code size, on an average.

data.question: Why is exhaustive search infeasible for LLVM Oz phase ordering?
data.answer_hint: LLVM Oz has 90 transformation passes, 54 unique, creating about 54^90 or 10^156 possible sequences.
data.expected_chunk_text: We use a reinforcement learning approach as the search space of optimizations is too big to enumerate. For a compiler with m optimization passes, if the sequence length is fixed as n, then there can be potentially mn combinations, allowing repetitions. And, Oz of LLVM has 90 transformation passes, among which 54 are unique. If one has to replicate such a setup, there can be potentially 5490 ≈10156 combinations.As it can be seen, creating this many combinations for each ele- ment of the dataset is highly infeasible, making reinforcement learning a go to method for this problem.

data.question: What are the key contributions of POSET-RL?
data.answer_hint: It proposes a DQN-based RL model using IR2Vec, ODG sub-sequences, multi-architecture evaluation, and improved size/runtime over Oz.
data.expected_chunk_text: Our trained model is able to reduce binary sizes for most of the benchmarks from MiBench [16], SPEC CPU (int and fp) 2017 [17], and SPEC CPU 2006 [18] benchmark-suites. Contributions: The following are our key contributions: • We propose a novel Deep Q-Network (DQN) based RL model that predicts the optimal sequence of optimization passes for a given input source program. Our framework is unique as it builds from embeddings given by IR2Vec encoding and derives its scalability characteristics. •We propose a Oz Dependence Graph (ODG) to clus- ter the individual optimization passes into various sub- sequences that forms an action space of the RL model.

data.question: Why does POSET-RL use IR2Vec representations?
data.answer_hint: IR2Vec captures syntax and semantics better than expert-designed features and represents programs as states.
data.expected_chunk_text: As it can be seen, creating this many combinations for each ele- ment of the dataset is highly infeasible, making reinforcement learning a go to method for this problem. Also, we use IR2Vec representations as it captures the syntax and semantics of the code intricately when compared to the expert-designed feature set. IR2Vec representations of the program are modelled as the state, and the sequence of optimization passes as actions for the formulated RL problem.The agent predicts the optimizations to apply on the input program.

data.question: How are agent, states, action, and environment defined in the RL background section?
data.answer_hint: Agent chooses actions, states represent environment instances, actions are decisions, and environment rewards or punishes actions.
data.expected_chunk_text: A rein- forcement learning problem modelled on the Markov Decision Process usually consists of the following: • Agent: An agent is the actor that makes a decision by choosing an action from an action space for which it receives reward from the environment. A positive valued reward represents a good decision and negative value represents a bad decision. After performing any action, the agent moves from current state to the new state. • States: An instance of the environment is defined as a state.For each action the agent takes, the current state transitions to a new state. •

data.question: Why does the paper use Double DQN instead of vanilla DQN?
data.answer_hint: Double DQN reduces overestimation of Q-values by separating action selection and value estimation.
data.expected_chunk_text: The various flavours of DQN have been proposed and are widely used [13], [14], [19], [20]. We use Double DQN for modelling our problem. A vanilla DQN on training suffers from the overestimation of Q values [19]. The notion of double DQN was introduced to meet with this challenge.Instead of using the target network for predicting the future q-value, double DQN uses an online network to predict the best action a′ for a given state s′. The q-value, q(s′, a′) for the state-action pair (s′, a′) is given by the target network.

data.question: How is the reward R computed in POSET-RL?
data.answer_hint: Reward combines binary size improvement and throughput improvement weighted by alpha and beta.
data.expected_chunk_text: C. Reward Computation We define our reward (R) as the combination of improve- ment in binary size and the throughput corresponding to the optimized and the original, non optimized versions of the compiled object file. It is computed as: R = α ∗RBinSize + β ∗RT hroughput (1) Where, reward corresponding to BinSize (RBinSize) is defined as the ratio of change in size between each interim states of an episode to binary size of the original object file, as shown in Eqn (2).RBinSize = BinSizelast −BinSizecurr BinSizebase (2) Here, BinSizelast refers to the size of the object file of the previous state, BinSizecurr refers to the total size of the object file after performing the action chosen by the model, and BinSizebase refers to the size of the object file without any optimization.

data.question: What does RBinSize measure in the reward computation?
data.answer_hint: RBinSize measures change in object file size between states relative to the base unoptimized object file size.
data.expected_chunk_text: RBinSize = BinSizelast −BinSizecurr BinSizebase (2) Here, BinSizelast refers to the size of the object file of the previous state, BinSizecurr refers to the total size of the object file after performing the action chosen by the model, and BinSizebase refers to the size of the object file without any optimization.We describe the throughput component of the reward, RT hroughput as the ratio of change in throughput between each interim states of an episode to the throughput of the original object file, as shown in Eqn (3).

data.question: What does the throughput reward component measure?
data.answer_hint: It measures change in LLVM-MCA throughput between current and previous states relative to base throughput.
data.expected_chunk_text: We describe the throughput component of the reward, RT hroughput as the ratio of change in throughput between each interim states of an episode to the throughput of the original object file, as shown in Eqn (3). RT hroughput = Throughtputcurr −Throughtputlast Throughtputbase (3) Here, Throughputcurr, Throughputlast and Throughputbase refer to the throughput generated by LLVM-MCA for current state, last state and without any optimization respectively. IV.SELECTION OF SUB-SEQUENCES LLVM provides several optimization sequences (O0, O1, O2, O3, Os, and Oz) that contribute either or both to enhancing speedup and reduction of code size.

data.question: Why does the paper prefer sub-sequences over individual optimization passes?
data.answer_hint: Individual passes create huge search spaces and may violate dependencies or disturb IR properties.
data.expected_chunk_text: Finding the optimal order of optimization passes with repet- itive occurrences of optimizations has always been a very hard problem. One simple way to define action space is to make every individual pass as an action for our RL model. Firstly, this has potential to induce a very large search space, as it grows exponentially (due to various possible combinations of individual passes, and sequence lengths).Further, this type of modeling faces the following complications: • Certain transformation passes cannot be applied individ- ually.

data.question: How are manually grouped sub-sequences created?
data.answer_hint: They are created by grouping Oz passes based on functionality and their placement in the Oz sequence.
data.expected_chunk_text: Table I shows the Oz optimization sequence for llvm- 10. The sub-sequences created manually from LLVM's Oz optimization sequence are listed in Table II. This manual grouping of passes has been done by looking into specific functionalities of passes and the Oz sequence. For example, sequence 2 performs some module level optimizations, while sequence 4 performs inlining.Similarly, sequence 7 contains loop related passes like loop rotate, loop invariant code motion, loop unswitch and also combines redundant instructions, while sequence 12 performs loop distribution and vectorization.

data.question: What is the Oz Dependence Graph?
data.answer_hint: ODG is a directed graph derived from the ordering of passes in the Oz optimization sequence.
data.expected_chunk_text: Similarly, sequence 7 contains loop related passes like loop rotate, loop invariant code motion, loop unswitch and also combines redundant instructions, while sequence 12 performs loop distribution and vectorization. B. Sub-sequences generated by Oz Directed Graph (ODG) We utilize the ordering of the passes in Oz to create new sequences. We propose a directed graph (ODG) that is derived from the ordering of Oz passes, as shown in Fig. 4.Each transformation pass in the Oz flag shown in the Table I forms the node, and the edges depict the ordering of the passes within the sequence.

data.question: How does ODG generate sub-sequences for the action space?
data.answer_hint: It traverses from critical nodes and uses visited nodes as sub-sequences while preserving dependencies.
data.expected_chunk_text: 4: Oz Dependence Graph edge between two nodes if they appear consecutively in the Oz pass sequence. For example, as simplifycfg appears after instcombine in Oz, we create a directed edge from simplifycfg to instcombine in the graph. We pick nodes with a degree greater than an arbitrary value k as critical nodes. Such critical nodes are the prominent nodes in the graph, where more than k passes depend on them. Hence, we start and end the traversal on the ODG with such nodes.ODG, when traversed from a critical node, would result in a walk where the dependencies of each pass would appear before it.

data.question: What LLVM version and dataset are used in the experiments?
data.answer_hint: The experiments use LLVM-10 and 130 source files from LLVM-test-suite single-source benchmarks.
data.expected_chunk_text: The IR2Vec [11] encoding and infrastructure which we choose to represent input source programs also fits nicely with our choice for LLVM-10. We use 130 source code files from the single source bench- marks of LLVM-test-suite [15]. And, we consider entirely different set of programs/benchmarks from MiBench, LLVM- test-suite, SPEC-CPU 2006 and SPEC-CPU-2017 benchmark suite for validation.We use the program level embeddings of IR2Vec which represents a complete source program as a vector of 300 dimensions.

data.question: How many dimensions are used in the program-level IR2Vec embeddings?
data.answer_hint: Program-level IR2Vec embeddings represent a complete source program as a 300-dimensional vector.
data.expected_chunk_text: The IR2Vec [11] encoding and infrastructure which we choose to represent input source programs also fits nicely with our choice for LLVM-10. We use 130 source code files from the single source bench- marks of LLVM-test-suite [15]. And, we consider entirely different set of programs/benchmarks from MiBench, LLVM- test-suite, SPEC-CPU 2006 and SPEC-CPU-2017 benchmark suite for validation.We use the program level embeddings of IR2Vec which represents a complete source program as a vector of 300 dimensions.

data.question: How is the trained model evaluated on benchmarks?
data.answer_hint: They apply predicted optimization sequences, generate binaries, and compare binary size with Oz.
data.expected_chunk_text: To check the performance of our trained model, for the above mentioned benchmarks, we apply the optimization sequence as suggested by the trained model. Then, we generate the binaries and compute the binary size for all the programs. We then compare it with the binary size generated with Oz optimization for the respective program.In Table IV, we show the min, avg and max % of size reduction for both x86 and AArch64 processor when we use sequences predicted by the model that is trained with manual and ODG sub-sequences.

data.question: What does a negative size reduction value indicate in the results?
data.answer_hint: A negative value means the predicted sequence produced a larger binary than Oz.
data.expected_chunk_text: In Table IV, we show the min, avg and max % of size reduction for both x86 and AArch64 processor when we use sequences predicted by the model that is trained with manual and ODG sub-sequences. Negative value indicates that the binary size obtained by applying the predicted sequence is more than the binary size obtained using Oz. Min represents the % of the minimum size improvement. Or, if it is negative, it represents the maximum size increased.Max represents the maximum size reduction, while avg represents average/mean size reduction for the respective benchmark.

data.question: Why are different predicted sub-sequences useful for different sources?
data.answer_hint: Different sources receive different predicted sequences, which improves performance.
data.expected_chunk_text: It can also be seen that different sub-sequences are predicted for different sources, that in turn improves the performance. VI. RELATED WORKS In recent days, several approaches that use machine learning to get better heuristics for many compiler optimization prob- lems like vectorization [24] [25], inlining [26], unrolling [27], etc. have been proposed and are widely accepted. Several approaches have been proposed to use machine learning techniques to solve the phase ordering problem.These works range from using genetic algorithms [2], [28], clustering techniques [4], and Bayesian networks [29].

data.question: Why does reinforcement learning fit compiler phase ordering naturally?
data.answer_hint: Phase ordering has too many possible optimization combinations, making RL a natural fit.
data.expected_chunk_text: code2vec uses the paths of Abstract Syntax Trees, while inst2vec and IR2Vec use the Intermediate Representation of the programs to form language independent representations. These approaches come up with representations that are inde- pendent of the underlying applications. Several approaches that use DNNs on reinforcement learn- ing setup have been proposed to solve the phase ordering problem [6], [32].Modelling phase ordering for compiler optimizations as a reinforcement learning fits naturally, as the number of possible combinations of the optimizations are too high even on fixing the sequence length.

data.question: What is a compiler according to the thesis?
data.answer_hint: A compiler reads a program in one language and translates it into an equivalent program in another language.
data.expected_chunk_text: This thesis focuses on the applica- tions of machine learning in optimizing compilers, an overview of the gradual advancement of the Machine Learning in Compiler Optimization domain. 1.2 Understanding the Compiler What is a compiler? In simple terms, Compilers can read a program in one language and translate it into an equivalent program in another language.This process is often used to translate a program written in assembly or high-level language into an equivalent program written in machine language which could be executed by a computer [9].

data.question: What are the compiler front-end and back-end responsible for?
data.answer_hint: The front-end reads source and generates IR; the back-end converts it into optimized machine-readable code.
data.expected_chunk_text: To catch up with the gap, compilers needed to be more complex, and new algorithms needed to be implemented to be optimized for the new architectures [9]. To understand the tasks of a compiler, it's useful to talk about each funda- mental block inside a compiler. A compiler could be divided into two regions [40].front-end, which is responsible for reading the source program and gen- erating an intermediate representation, and back-end, which is responsible for synthesizing the output from the front-end and converting it into optimized and accurate machine-readable code.

data.question: What are the three steps in the compiler front-end?
data.answer_hint: Lexical Analyzer, Syntax Analyzer, and Semantic Analyzer.
data.expected_chunk_text: Syntax Analyzer parses the tokens generated in the lexical analyzer to check if the tokens match with a given syntax of a language. This phase is carried out by building a syntax tree/parse tree and the syntax is defined by a grammar. Semantic Analyzer checks whether the declarations and statements in the input are semantically correct. Semantic analysis consists of three main functions. Type Checking, Label Checking and Flow Control Checking.The output of the front end is a semantic representation of the program.

data.question: What are the fundamental properties of compiler optimization algorithms?
data.answer_hint: Correctness, performance, usefulness, and manageability.
data.expected_chunk_text: Correctness: It needs to be accurate; this means that the results of the optimized code need to be equivalent to the results of the code when executing. 2. Performance: The compile time must be reasonable after adding the optimizing algorithm 3. Usefulness: The optimization algorithm should improve the performance of many programs. 4. Manageability: The effort to implement, and integrate should be man- ageable.1.3 Machine Learning in Compiler Optimization Machine Learning can be defined as a method of solving problems by training a model with a selected dataset and then applying the model to the existing prob- lems [46].

data.question: How does the thesis define machine learning?
data.answer_hint: Machine learning is solving problems by training a model with a selected dataset and applying it to existing problems.
data.expected_chunk_text: Correctness: It needs to be accurate; this means that the results of the optimized code need to be equivalent to the results of the code when executing. 2. Performance: The compile time must be reasonable after adding the optimizing algorithm 3. Usefulness: The optimization algorithm should improve the performance of many programs. 4. Manageability: The effort to implement, and integrate should be man- ageable.1.3 Machine Learning in Compiler Optimization Machine Learning can be defined as a method of solving problems by training a model with a selected dataset and then applying the model to the existing prob- lems [46].

data.question: How has compiler optimization ML evolved since 2002?
data.answer_hint: It evolved from predictive methods with adaptive steering algorithms to advanced approaches such as reinforcement neural networks and CNNs.
data.expected_chunk_text: From 2002 to the present the domain has been developed from discussing predictive methods in compiler op- timizations with a steering algorithm that adapts after each compilation round [4] towards using more advanced machine learning concepts like Reinforcement Neural Networks [56] and Convolutional Neural Networks [37]. When analyzing the development, it's clear that the development of the machine learning domain has been directly affected by the advancements in compiler optimization.1.4 Structure of the Thesis The purpose of the thesis is to provide an overview of the applications of ma- chine learning in compiler optimizations.

data.question: What is the purpose of the thesis?
data.answer_hint: To provide an overview of machine learning applications in compiler optimization.
data.expected_chunk_text: From 2002 to the present the domain has been developed from discussing predictive methods in compiler op- timizations with a steering algorithm that adapts after each compilation round [4] towards using more advanced machine learning concepts like Reinforcement Neural Networks [56] and Convolutional Neural Networks [37]. When analyzing the development, it's clear that the development of the machine learning domain has been directly affected by the advancements in compiler optimization.1.4 Structure of the Thesis The purpose of the thesis is to provide an overview of the applications of ma- chine learning in compiler optimizations.

data.question: What future approach does the thesis introduce after the state-of-the-art chapter?
data.answer_hint: It explores conceptual multi-agent reinforcement learning approaches for phase ordering using CompilerGym.
data.expected_chunk_text: The next part of the thesis will be carried out by exploring two conceptual approaches suggested by the author to improve the Phase Ordering Problem with Reinforcement Learning, the intention of the chapter being to in- troduce a Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning environment into the compiler optimization domain and provide the reader the knowledge on how to integrate Machine Learning with compiler optimizations by implementing the method on the CompilerGym Environment.In the Comparison chapter, the improvement and the validity of the two approaches are discussed under different benchmarks.

data.question: What is the phase ordering problem?
data.answer_hint: It is searching for the optimal sequence of optimization methods to improve a program's speed, code size, or power consumption.
data.expected_chunk_text: benchmarks. benchmarks. Additionally, the model has improved on the number of Loop- Vectorization and number of L1 Cache hits on the TSVC, PolyBench, and PolyBench-NN datasets. 3.3.4 Phase Ordering Phase Ordering problem could be defined as searching for the optimal possible sequence of optimization methods that will improve a certain program towards faster execution, reduced code size, or reduced power consumption.By ana- lyzing the characteristics of the problem, it is visible that the Phase Ordering problem could be seamlessly mapped into a Reinforcement Learning environ- ment. (

data.question: Why was the phase ordering problem difficult for standard heuristics?
data.answer_hint: It is NP-hard and one solution cannot be applied to all programs.
data.expected_chunk_text: As the number of optimiza- tion methods increased, the complexity of the problem increased as well. This NP-hard problem of selecting the best sequence of compiler optimization for a given program was not possible to solve with standard heuristic methods and one solution could not be applied to all the programs. Hence, the community leaned toward different approaches to solve this problem.As a solution, Genetic Algorithm (GA) approaches have been discussed in many research journals since the early 20th century. [

data.question: How did genetic algorithms map natural terms to phase ordering?
data.answer_hint: Genes map to one optimization, chromosomes to optimization sequences, fitness to code reduction, and population to chromosomes.
data.expected_chunk_text: He has addressed the solution by defining natural terms with the terms of the problem at hand. 1. Genes - Mapped with one Optimization method 2. Chromosomes - Mapped with a sequence of twelve Optimization methods 3. Fitness - Mapped with the reduction rate of the code for a Chromosome 4.Population - Mapped with the number of chromosomes in a given time As the process of the GA, the chromosome with the best fitness values was selected by removing the unfit chromosomes and applying crossover operations on the best-fit chromosomes for 100 cycles or, as in Evolutionary terms, for 100 generations.

data.question: What improvement did the GA approach provide compared to the default optimization sequence?
data.answer_hint: It provided 40% smaller and 25% faster code compared to the default sequence.
data.expected_chunk_text: Population - Mapped with the number of chromosomes in a given time As the process of the GA, the chromosome with the best fitness values was selected by removing the unfit chromosomes and applying crossover operations on the best-fit chromosomes for 100 cycles or, as in Evolutionary terms, for 100 generations. This approach provided 40% smaller and 25% faster code compared to the default optimizing sequence in the compiler.Similarly to this approach, [7] and [6] discussed a Genetic Approach to solving the Phase Ordering prob- lem.

data.question: What was the 2007 performance-counter approach for selecting compiler optimizations?
data.answer_hint: It trained a model mapping performance counter features to good optimizations.
data.expected_chunk_text: Using prior information in se- lecting a sequence [10] could be considered as the main advantage in leaning into the machine learning approaches. In 2007, [10] introduced an approach to select good compiler optimizations using performance counters with training a model. They have constructed a model of x →t, with x being the feature set of performance counters and t being the set of good optimizations.The model is then trained with training data to search for optimization methods that im- prove performance.

data.question: How did MiCOMP mitigate phase ordering complexity?
data.answer_hint: MiCOMP predicted complete optimization sequences from application features using offline supervised training and online prediction.
data.expected_chunk_text: 18] is based on a statistical methodology to infer the probability distribution of the compiler op- timizations to achieve the best performance. The Bayesian Network was trained with a set of applications based on software characteristics as the features to learn. Later in [22], the author has developed the approach into a framework. In [25], the Phase Ordering problem was discussed using optimization sub- sequences and ML via a framework, MiCOMP.This paper discussed mitigating the complexity of the Phase Ordering problem by predicting the complete op- timization sequence by looking at the characteristics of a program.

data.question: What RL algorithms were discussed for HLS phase ordering?
data.answer_hint: DQN and Policy Gradient were used for phase ordering in HLS programs.
data.expected_chunk_text: With time, the compiler community has explored beyond the Supervised Learning approaches to different ML techniques like Reinforcement Learning (RL) and Graph Neural Networks in compiler optimization context. The Phase Ordering problem was discussed with RL approaches in [33] - 2019 [35] - 2020 and [47] - 2022. In [33], they have discussed using two types of RL algorithms (DQN - Deep Q Networks, PG - Policy Gradient) for the Phase Ordering prob- lems in the HLS programs.The results were compared with -03 for 3, 12, and 24 optimizations for a sequence.

data.question: What are the three application categories of ML in compiler optimization?
data.answer_hint: Specific optimization methods, general optimization improvement methods, and improvements to ML techniques used in compilers.
data.expected_chunk_text: ML approaches to improve optimization methods in general - These papers discuss their approaches in general, even though the research was carried out with one or two optimization problems these papers pro- pose a method that could apply to other optimization problems. 3. Improvements for the Machine Learning Techniques Used in Compilers - These research papers propose frameworks and guidelines to improve the integration of Machine Learning techniques.3.2.1 Machine Learning for a Specific Optimization Method One way of integrating ML with compiler optimization is to use ML to im- prove a specific problem.

data.question: What is CompilerGym?
data.answer_hint: CompilerGym is an environment that lets users integrate reinforcement learning into compiler optimization tasks.
data.expected_chunk_text: The next part of the thesis will be carried out by exploring two conceptual approaches suggested by the author to improve the Phase Ordering Problem with Reinforcement Learning, the intention of the chapter being to in- troduce a Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning environment into the compiler optimization domain and provide the reader the knowledge on how to integrate Machine Learning with compiler optimizations by implementing the method on the CompilerGym Environment.In the Comparison chapter, the improvement and the validity of the two approaches are discussed under different benchmarks.

data.question: What is MLGO?
data.answer_hint: MLGO is a framework for integrating ML techniques into an industrial compiler, LLVM.
data.expected_chunk_text: Few research publi- cations [52] [26] have already started using CompilerGym as their experimental environment and utilized the benchmarks available. Another framework implemented towards facilitating the researchers' re- quirements is MLGO [42]. MLGO introduces a framework to integrate Ma- chine Learning techniques in an industrial compiler (LLVM). The framework uses two main ML algorithms, Policy Gradient and Evolution strategies.These frameworks provide experimental environments for researchers to investigate Compiler Optimization in the Machine Learning space with ease of use and high extensibility.

data.question: What is the register allocation problem?
data.answer_hint: It assigns a finite set of registers to an unbounded set of variables.
data.expected_chunk_text: To find the answers, we explore current progress and the latest research on significant optimization methods and the improvements made with Machine Learning in recent years. 3.3.1 Register Allocation Register Allocation problem is an important and heavily studied problem among the optimization methods. The problem could be described as assigning a finite set of registers to an unbounded set of variables [57].The goal of this opti- mization method is to use the processor's registers more efficiently to minimize slow memory accesses.

data.question: What results did RL4REAL report for SPEC CPU 2017 and 2006?
data.answer_hint: RL4REAL reported about 7% runtime improvement on SPEC CPU 2017 and 5% on SPEC CPU 2006.
data.expected_chunk_text: The experiments of this paper were implemented in the LLVM-gRPC for integration with ML models, and SPEC CPU 2006 and 2017 benchmark suites were used to compare the results. In the SPEC CPU 2017, the RL4REAL [57] displayed around 7% improvement in the run time while LLVM's Greedy allocator shows 6% improvement, which emphasizes around 1% improvement on the greedy allocator from LLVM.For the SPEC CPU 2006, the RL4REAL marked a 5% runtime improvement, while the greedy allocator marked no significant improvement.

data.question: What is loop unrolling?
data.answer_hint: Loop unrolling replaces a loop body k times, where k is the unrolling factor.
data.expected_chunk_text: 3.3.2 Loop Unrolling Loop Unrolling is the methodology of replacing a loop body k-number of times in the program, given that k is the unrolling factor. This process reduces the overhead by decreasing the number of branch operations in a program. A pri- mary advantage of Loop Unrolling is it opens the ability to Instruction Level Parallelism to the compiler. This optimization is vital to architectures that have high branching overhead[8].However, predicting an erroneous unrolling factor could lead to increased cache misses, register pressure, and memory inefficien- cies [50].

data.question: What ensemble methods were used to predict loop unroll factors?
data.answer_hint: XGBoost, Random Forest, and Bagging.
data.expected_chunk_text: Figure 3.4: Bagging strategy in Ensemble Learning Bagging strategy, and Bagging to predict the unroll factors of a program. Figure 3.4: Bagging strategy in Ensemble Learning Bagging strategy, and Bagging to predict the unroll factors of a program. More than 200 programs were gathered for the training data, including PolyBench and Shootout. This paper has stated achieving a precision factor of 99.56% in identifying the Unroll Factor in the labeled dataset.Additionally, the results re- flect that the XGBoost and Random Forest methods outperformed the Bagging method in predicting the unroll factor for the used datasets.

data.question: What is loop distribution?
data.answer_hint: Loop distribution breaks a single loop into two or more loops over distinct subsets of the original loop body.
data.expected_chunk_text: In Loop Distribution, a single loop will be broken up into two or multiple loops, each of which iterates over a distinct subset of the statements in the body of the original loop. The Loop Distribution should always ensures the perseverance of the data and the control dependencies of the original loop [2]. Other than the parellelizability of the loop, Loop Distribution enables the vectorizability of the loop and the locality of the loop[48].Modern architectures with SIMD vector registers allow a unique form of parallelization, which is called vectorization.

data.question: What does CompilerGym provide for ML compiler optimization experiments?
data.answer_hint: It provides datasets, reward spaces, observation spaces, and action spaces.
data.expected_chunk_text: These frameworks provide experimental environments for researchers to investigate Compiler Optimization in the Machine Learning space with ease of use and high extensibility. Characteristics of these frameworks are, that they provide Datasets, Reward Spaces, Observation Spaces, and Action Spaces to the users to carry out the implementation of new methodologies on top of their frameworks.3.3 State of the Art Application of Machine Learning in Optimizing Compilers is a broad and deep branch of research for the compiler community.
